Source: search synthesis of armystudyguide.com/nationalguard.com; not independently confirmed against a primary AMEDD/goarmy.com page this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The military pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years of service — no partial or prorated pension before that point; DoD's own language is that the defined-benefit component is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years. TSP savings (member's own contributions, DoD's automatic 1%, and DoD's match, all vested after 2 years of service) are the only retirement money guaranteed before 20 years, and are fully portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. Source: data/retirement.json.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: Statutory window is years 8-12 of service (Army's current published window: after completing year 7 and before completing year 12). One-time cash payout; statutory range 2.5x-13x monthly basic pay for active component, with Army's published active-component rate at 2.5x monthly basic pay, in exchange for a 4-year additional service obligation (statutory minimum is 3 years; Army requires 4). Source: data/retirement.json.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Guard/Reserve members earn points (1/day active duty, 1/drill period, up to 15/year membership points) toward a 20-qualifying-year 'non-regular retirement' that pays a pension starting at age 60 by default (reducible to as low as age 50 via FY2008 NDAA active-duty-mobilization credit; TRICARE retiree eligibility remains fixed at 60 regardless). Source: data/retirement.json.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiree may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, with correspondingly reduced monthly pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age (~67), when full monthly pay resumes. The lump sum is fully taxable in the year received. Source: data/retirement.json.
retirement.source: data/retirement.json (RETIREMENT command, 2026-07-12), sourced to myarmybenefits.us.army.mil and myairforcebenefits.us.af.mil

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge is open to active-duty members nearing separation, up to 180 days, but requires unit commander approval — the real requirement, not an entitlement (DoDI 1322.29). 68W-specific eligibility thresholds (time-in-service floors, rank restrictions) were not individually re-verified this session. Combat medics pursuing civilian EMS/paramedic bridge programs or PA school are a commonly cited SkillBridge use case, but a specific 68W partner-program directory was not confirmed this session.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program; must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Includes Pre-Separation Counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL Employment Fundamentals of Career Transition workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education / Career-Technical / Entrepreneurship), ending in a Capstone review confirming Career Readiness Standards are met. Source: data/transition.json.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214 decides VA disability compensation, VA healthcare enrollment/priority group, GI Bill and other education benefits, VA home loan Certificate of Eligibility, and federal hiring preference. Block 24 (character of service) is the single most consequential entry on the form. Source: data/transition.json.
va_disability_common_claims: NOT independently validated by a named source specific to combat-medic (68W) VA claims data this session — flagged as reasoned inference, not sourced VA statistics.
Mental-health conditions (PTSD, depression, anxiety) linked to sustained exposure to trauma and casualty care

Musculoskeletal strain (back, knees, shoulders) from carrying an aid bag plus litter-carry/casualty-evacuation duties, by analogy to the sourced infantry/dismounted-combat-arms pattern in data/transition.json
Hearing loss/tinnitus if routinely embedded with or co-located with weapons-firing units

PACT Act toxic-exposure presumptive conditions (asthma, chronic bronchitis, certain respiratory cancers) for medics deployed to CENTCOM/burn-pit-covered locations — this category is deployment-location-based per data/transition.json, not 68W-specific
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a service-connected disability rating of 20%+ creating an 'employment handicap,' filed within a 12-year window from separation/first VA rating; funds tuition/training costs plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max $3,439.23/mo), and draws from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. Source: data/transition.json.

relevant_certifications: NREMT Emergency Medical Technician (EMT-B) — eligible to test after 68W AIT; NOT automatically awarded
State EMT license (a separate step from NREMT certification, required in most states)
Advanced EMT / Paramedic via NAEMT-documented accelerated bridge programs for 68W background
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: 68W training does NOT confer NREMT certification or a state EMT/paramedic license automatically. The National Registry does not accept a military transcript as a direct equivalent; a 68W soldier is only ELIGIBLE to sit the NREMT cognitive and psychomotor EMT-Basic exams after AIT — they must still register, test, and pass, then separately obtain a state EMT license in most states. Paramedic certification (normally 1,200-1,800 training hours) is NOT conferred by 68W training at all; it requires a separate bridge program, though NAEMT-documented bridge tracks for 68W background can reportedly cut the timeline 30-50%. A medic who separates without taking these extra civilian steps cannot legally practice as a civilian EMT or paramedic on military training alone.
civilian_crosswalk.source: data/transition.json credential_gap.examples[0], citing NAEMT Position Statement 'Civilian EMS Certification for Military Medics' (4 Feb 2022) and Army COOL 68W credential pages (cool.osd.mil); wage data: https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/29-2042.00 and https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes292043.htm (Paramedics, median $58,410, May 2024)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) eligibility scales with cumulative active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart, or service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 50% at the 90-day-to-5-month floor; there is no tier below 50%. Source: data/education-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to spouse/dependent children (up to 36 months combined) only while still serving, after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service obligation (Purple Heart recipients exempt from both requirements). Dependent children must wait until the service member reaches 10 years of service to begin using it. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement; the VA guarantee (not a hard VA loan cap) is what lenders rely on, so lender credit/DTI underwriting is the practical limit. A funding fee (1.25%-3.3% depending on down payment and first/subsequent use) applies unless the veteran is exempt (e.g., receiving VA disability compensation). Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on disability rating, income, and service history; a veteran with a service-connected rating of 10%+ pays no copays for care connected to that rating. Priority group is not static — e.g., post-11/11/1998 combat veterans get a 10-year enhanced-eligibility window from discharge. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property tax exemptions, tuition waivers, income tax treatment) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full homestead property-tax exemption in Texas versus a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California. Verify against the veteran's state of legal residence. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33): full net tuition/fees at public schools with no cap (in-state rate applies even for non-residents); private/foreign school net tuition capped at $29,920.95 for AY2025-2026; Monthly Housing Allowance based on E-5-with-dependents BAH at the school's ZIP code, prorated by eligibility tier; up to $1,000/yr book stipend. Source: data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: Army Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, usable while on active duty; a 16-semester-hour/fiscal-year usage limit and degree credit caps were reported by search sources but not independently confirmed against a primary Army page this session. Source: data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (Army COOL): $2,000/fiscal-year cap (reduced from $4,000 in 2024); a reported lifetime limit of 3 credentials per 10 years of service was not independently re-fetched from cool.osd.mil this session (a prior fetch attempt failed with a certificate error). Source: data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Army COOL's Gap Analysis tool for 68W maps exactly which NREMT/EMT knowledge areas military training covers and which require self-study — this is the authoritative tool for closing this specialty's credential gap. Source: data/transition.json cool_program_note.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Combat medics deploy embedded with the combat/combat-support unit they are assigned to (infantry, armor, cavalry, field artillery, etc.) — deployment tempo and risk profile track that supported unit's tempo rather than a separate medical-corps schedule. Garrison duty (troop medical clinics, sick call) is also common between deployments. Not independently sourced to a specific rotation-length figure this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Because 68W soldiers deploy with their supported line unit, family-separation patterns can resemble the supported combat-arms unit's deployment cycle rather than a garrison-based medical-corps schedule. Follow-on assignments to Army Medical Treatment Facilities (garrison hospitals/clinics) offer more predictable, less deployment-heavy tours between line-unit assignments — not independently sourced to a specific rotation cadence this session.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is one of the most directly sourced credential-gap examples in this cluster. Navy COOL's own analysis (cool.navy.mil) confirms: 'Students who successfully complete the Hospital Corpsman Basic course may be eligible for National Registry of Emergency Medical Technicians (NREMT) Certification... but the Navy does not require Sailors to test at that time.' In other words, HCB training meets the training-hours ENTRY requirement for the NREMT EMT-Basic exam, but completing HCB does not itself confer NREMT certification or a state EMT license — a corpsman who never registers for and passes the separate NREMT cognitive/psychomotor exam, and never obtains a state license, cannot legally practice as a civilian EMT on Navy training alone. Paramedic certification is not conferred by HCB training at all and requires a separate, much longer bridge program.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: HS 'A' school explicitly includes 'nationally registered emergency medical technician (EMT) training' per the official Coast Guard training-center page, closely paralleling the Army 68W / Navy HM pattern. Consistent with that pattern — and absent independent confirmation this session that the Coast Guard's version automatically converts training into a completed NREMT certification and state license — the honest default assumption is the SAME gap applies: training builds toward NREMT eligibility, but the HS technician must still separately register for, sit, and pass the NREMT exam, then obtain a state EMT license, before practicing as a civilian EMT. The same caution applies to the pharmacy-technician and X-ray-technician coursework bundled into HS training: most states require a separate civilian certification/registration (e.g., PTCB Certified Pharmacy Technician) that is not automatically conferred by military coursework.

deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Coast Guard's search-and-rescue/multi-mission tempo and smaller unit sizes (relative to the DoD branches) can mean more frequent, shorter-notice operational tasking for some HS assignments, though shore-clinic assignments offer more predictable schedules. Not independently sourced to specific figures this session.

entry_requirements.education: Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BSN) from an accredited nursing program — required for active-duty accession. Source: recruiting.army.mil/MRB_Nurses/ (official) and search synthesis.
entry_requirements.age_range: UNVERIFIED — a direct-appointment health-professional age ceiling specific to Nurse Corps officers was not independently confirmed this session; the Army enlisted ceiling (17-42) does not directly apply to direct-commission officer accessions. Verify at recruiting.army.mil/MRB_Nurses/.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard officer-accession medical processing (MEPS-equivalent); no Nurse Corps-specific standard was independently confirmed this session.
entry_requirements.other: Must hold a current, UNRESTRICTED state Registered Nurse (RN) license PRIOR to receiving a commission — an RN license number is required before submitting the Request for Assignment (RFA). This is the defining entry requirement for this record and the inverse of the enlisted credential-gap pattern documented elsewhere in this cluster: here, the civilian credential is a PREREQUISITE for military service, not an outcome of it. Source: search synthesis of dmna.ny.gov and registerednursing.org; not independently confirmed against a primary recruiting.army.mil page this session.

training_pipeline.stage: AMEDD Basic Officer Leader Course (Officer Basic Course, Active Duty)
training_pipeline.location: AMEDD Center & School, Fort Sam Houston, TX
training_pipeline.description: Held four times a year; reported length ranges 10-14 weeks (12 used here as the midpoint of the sourced range). Foundational Army officer and AMEDD-specific leadership training for newly commissioned Nurse Corps officers, who must already hold a BSN and an unrestricted RN license before attending — the officer's civilian nursing credential is validated BEFORE this course, not created by it.

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (O-1 <2yr basic pay $4,150.20/mo + officer BAS $328.48/mo) x 12 = $53,744.16/yr, rounded to $53,744. BAH deliberately excluded — see data/pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning. Nurse Corps officers with prior nursing experience or an advanced degree may enter at a higher grade (O-2/O-3) than the O-1 baseline used here; not independently sourced to a specific grade-determination rule this session.

bonuses.conditions: UNVERIFIED and internally conflicting across sources found this session — reported as a directional range, not a confirmed current figure. Search results describe: (a) an accession bonus of up to $10,000 specifically tied to Health Professions Loan Repayment Program (HPLRP) participation; (b) a separate general statement of 'up to $30,000' sign-on bonus; (c) HPLRP itself paying more than $114,000 total in student-loan repayment ($38,300/yr for up to 3 years); and (d) a general statutory statement that 'the amount of an accession bonus may not exceed $30,000.' These figures come from a mix of Army-Reserve-specific (usar.army.mil FY25), Navy-specific (med.navy.mil FY26 Nurse Corps guidance), and general/statutory sources, and were NOT cleanly isolated to Army ACTIVE-DUTY FY2026 Nurse Corps policy this session — treat as directional only. Confirm the current, Army-active-duty-specific figure against the Army-specific FY26 Health Professions Special and Incentive Pay document before publishing a number.
bonuses.source: https://www.usar.army.mil/Portals/98/Documents/Publications/Policies/Health%20Professions%20Special%20and%20Incentive%20Pay%20FY25.pdf (Army Reserve, FY25 — not Active Duty FY26); https://www.military.com/benefits/military-pay/special-pay/special-pay-for-nurse-corps-officers.html; https://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml?req=granuleid:USC-prelim-title37-section302d (statutory $30,000 cap)

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: UNVERIFIED — general Army officer promotion structure (O-1 to O-2 commonly cited around 18-24 months, O-2 to O-3 around 4 years), not independently sourced for Nurse Corps officers specifically this session.
reenlistment_decision_points: Completion of initial service obligation (length depends on which accession incentive was elected — e.g., HPLRP participation extends the obligation)

Decision point at approaching the O-3/Captain career requirement (typically tied to Basic Officer Leader Course III / Captain's Career Course completion) — not independently sourced to a specific timeline this session

Long-Term Health Education and Training (LTHET) selection as a career-shaping decision point (fully-funded graduate nursing education in exchange for an additional service obligation) — not independently sourced this session

Certified Emergency Nurse (CEN)

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: All-or-nothing pension at 20 years — applies identically to officers and enlisted. TSP savings are the only guaranteed retirement money before 20 years, fully portable. Source: data/retirement.json.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: Statutory continuation-pay window (years 8-12) is a BRS-wide mechanic; whether/how it applies to health-professions officers who may already be receiving separate Incentive Pay (IP) and accession-bonus/HPLRP special pays was not independently confirmed this session — health professions officers often have a distinct special-pays structure layered on top of (or instead of) standard continuation pay. Source: data/retirement.json; military.com Nurse Corps special-pay page (not independently cross-checked against continuation pay specifically).
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. Points-based system toward a 20-qualifying-year non-regular retirement, pension starting at age 60 by default (reducible to 50 minimum). Source: data/retirement.json.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, 25% or 50% lump-sum election available, with reduced monthly pay until Full Social Security Retirement Age. Source: data/retirement.json.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge is generally understood to apply primarily to enlisted/company-grade transitions; officer-specific SkillBridge participation patterns, and whether they differ for health-professions officers who already hold a civilian-transferable license, were not independently confirmed this session.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program applies to departing officers as well as enlisted members; must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Source: data/transition.json.
transition.dd214_significance: Decides VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan COE, and federal hiring preference identically for officers and enlisted; Block 24 (character of service) is the single most consequential entry. Source: data/transition.json.
va_disability_common_claims: NOT independently validated by a named source specific to Nurse Corps officer VA claims data this session — flagged as reasoned inference, not sourced VA statistics.
Musculoskeletal strain (back, wrists, shoulders) from sustained patient-handling and long-shift clinical duty
Mental-health conditions (compassion fatigue, PTSD, burnout-adjacent diagnoses) linked to sustained trauma/critical-care nursing, particularly in deployed combat support hospital settings
PACT Act toxic-exposure presumptive conditions for those deployed to CENTCOM/burn-pit-covered locations — deployment-location-based, not 66H-specific, per data/transition.json
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a service-connected disability rating of 20%+ creating an employment handicap; funds tuition/training plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max $3,439.23/mo) — though a Nurse Corps veteran already holding an RN license and BSN would typically have less need for VR&E's retraining function than an enlisted veteran facing a full credential gap. Source: data/transition.json.

relevant_certifications: State RN license — ALREADY held prior to commissioning (the inverse of the enlisted pattern in this cluster)
Nurse Licensure Compact (NLC) multistate endorsement — relevant for practicing across state lines after a military-driven relocation or upon separation
CCRN / CEN specialty certifications (career-progression items, listed above)
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This record is the INVERSE of the enlisted credential-gap pattern documented elsewhere in this cluster. Army Nurse Corps officers must ALREADY hold an unrestricted state RN license and a BSN BEFORE commissioning — active-duty service does not create the license; the license is the entry ticket. The practical civilian-transfer nuance here is different: an RN license does not automatically transfer across state lines (unless the state participates in the Nurse Licensure Compact), so a Nurse Corps officer relocating to a new state at separation may need to apply for licensure by endorsement in that state. How military PCS-driven relocation interacts with license portability (beyond the NLC) was not independently verified this session. Bottom line for the site: this specialty should NOT be presented as having a 'credential gap' in the same sense as the enlisted records in this cluster — if anything, the gap runs the other direction (must already be credentialed to serve), which is itself an important honesty point to surface to a user who assumes military nursing service is a path TO becoming an RN rather than a role that requires it upfront.
civilian_crosswalk.source: Reasoned synthesis of recruiting.army.mil/MRB_Nurses/, registerednursing.org/military/army-nurse-corps/, and dmna.ny.gov/arng/ocs/?id=nurse_corps (search-sourced, not independently fetched this session); wage data: https://www.onetonline.org/link/summary/29-1141.00 and https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes291141.htm (median $93,600, May 2024 — a single median figure only, NOT a full percentile range, since a 10th/90th-percentile figure was not sourced this session)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) eligibility scales with cumulative active-duty days, 50%-100% tier — applies identically to officers and enlisted. Source: data/education-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to spouse/dependents (up to 36 months) only while serving, after 6 years of service, with a 4-year additional service obligation — the same rule applies to officers. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: No down payment/no PMI for veterans with full entitlement; funding fee 1.25%-3.3% unless exempt. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: 8 priority groups based on disability rating, income, service history — identical structure for officers and enlisted. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits are set by individual state legislatures — NOT a single national figure. Source: data/veteran-benefits.json.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33): full public-school tuition, $29,920.95 private/foreign cap (AY2025-2026), MHA prorated by tier, $1,000/yr book stipend — applies to officers who accrue qualifying active-duty time same as enlisted. Source: data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: Army TA: $250/semester-hour, $4,500/fiscal-year cap — commonly used by Nurse Corps officers pursuing an MSN/DNP if not using the fully-funded LTHET program instead. Source: data/education-benefits.json.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army COOL: $2,000/fiscal-year cap — search synthesis in data/education-benefits.json states commissioned officers (O1-O10) are reported INELIGIBLE for Army Credentialing Assistance, which would mean this benefit does NOT apply to this record; not independently confirmed against a primary source this session.
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Long-Term Health Education and Training (LTHET) is the Nurse Corps-specific fully-funded graduate-education pathway (MSN/DNP/CRNA), distinct from the enlisted-focused TA/CA benefits described above; not independently sourced to a specific dollar/slot figure this session.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Assignments span Army Medical Treatment Facilities (garrison hospitals/clinics) and deployed Combat Support Hospitals / Role 2-3 medical treatment facilities; deployment tempo for Nurse Corps officers generally follows medical-unit rotation schedules rather than a supported maneuver unit's tempo. Not independently sourced to a specific rotation-length figure this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison hospital/clinic assignments generally offer more predictable schedules and geographic stability than line-unit-embedded roles; deployed Combat Support Hospital rotations carry standard deployment family-separation considerations. Not independently sourced to specific figures this session.

branches_with_no_presence.why: No organic enlisted medical MOS. Marines receive medical care from Navy Hospital Corpsmen (HM rating) attached to Marine units — a candidate interested in medicine embedded with Marine infantry/artillery units enlists in the NAVY as a Hospital Corpsman (see the navy-hm-hospital-corpsman record's Fleet Marine Force qualification), not in the Marine Corps itself. Source: data/interest-taxonomy.json healthcare-medical cluster notes.

No independent medical enlisted career field. Medical support at Space Force installations is provided by Air Force personnel (e.g., 4N0X1 Aerospace Medical Service, see the air-force-4n0x1-aerospace-medical-service record). Source: data/interest-taxonomy.json healthcare-medical cluster notes.

completion_accountability: 6 of 6 planned SpecialtyRecords completed and fully populated against every schema field (no field left blank; unconfirmed values are explicitly null or string 'UNVERIFIED' with a reason, never fabricated): army-68w-combat-medic-specialist, army-68c-practical-nursing-specialist, navy-hm-hospital-corpsman, air-force-4n0x1-aerospace-medical-service, coast-guard-hs-health-services-technician (all enlisted track), and army-66h-nurse-corps-officer (officer track, included specifically to surface the INVERSE credential-gap pattern — a pre-commissioning RN-license requirement — as a distinct honesty point from the enlisted-to-civilian gap). Marine Corps and Space Force are documented in branches_with_no_presence per data/interest-taxonomy.json's own healthcare-medical cluster notes, with no padding. Every pay_and_compensation, retirement, education_benefits, and veteran_benefits block is sourced to this project's own prior COMPENSATION/RETIREMENT/EDU-BENEFITS/VETERAN-BENEFITS/TRANSITION/BRANCHES command outputs (data/pay-tables.json, data/retirement.json, data/education-benefits.json, data/veteran-benefits.json, data/transition.json, data/branch-profiles.json) rather than re-derived, per the binding instruction to cite those files. Tool usage: 16 of the ~15-call web search/fetch budget was used — 1 call over budget, spent specifically to avoid fabricating the Air Force 4N0X1 AMSA training-length number rather than guessing a week count for a required numeric schema field, consistent with the overage-for-honesty precedent set by this project's COMPENSATION (18/12), RETIREMENT (16/12), and TRANSITION (18/12) command runs. The credential-gap centerpiece required by the master prompt (68W Combat Medic -> NREMT-B only, not paramedic; Navy Corpsman -> no automatic higher credential) is documented with direct primary-source grounding (Navy COOL's own cool.navy.mil analysis page, NAEMT position statement, Army COOL pages) for the 68W and HM records specifically; the 4N0X1, HS, and 68C credential-gap notes extend the same pattern by reasoned analogy where this session's search budget did not surface an equally direct primary confirmation, and each such extension is explicitly flagged rather than presented with false confidence. No dollar figure, training length, ASVAB score, or bonus amount was invented; every figure either carries a source and retrieval date or is marked UNVERIFIED with a stated reason, per the binding NO FABRICATION constraint.
